questions.question: What is the mental health prevalence rate for youth elite athletes compared to the general population?

options.text: Youth elite athletes have much lower rates due to 'mental toughness'
16.9% current and 25.1% lifetime prevalence of mental disorders — comparable to or exceeding general population rates
Elite athletes never experience mental health issues
Mental health data isn't available for elite athletes

questions.explanation: Youth elite athletes show 16.9% CURRENT and 25.1% LIFETIME prevalence of mental disorders — rates comparable to or exceeding the general population. This contradicts the assumption that elite athletes are 'mentally tough' or protected by sport participation. The competitive environment, performance pressure, and social isolation actually INCREASE mental health risk at elite levels.

questions.question: What percentage of young people quit organized sports by age 15, and what is the primary reason?

options.text: 20-30% quit, primarily due to injury
40-50% quit, primarily due to lack of talent
70-80% quit, primarily due to PSYCHOLOGICAL factors (stress, reduced enjoyment, performance pressure)
Less than 10% quit — most continue through high school

questions.explanation: 70-80% of young people quit organized sports by age 15. The dominant predictor is NOT injury or logistics — it's PSYCHOLOGICAL factors: stress, reduced enjoyment, and performance pressure. This is a public health crisis in which competitive youth sport systems may be producing more harm than benefit for the majority of participants. This dropout rate represents a massive failure of sport systems to prioritize enjoyment and wellbeing.

questions.question: According to the 2026 Public Health Post review, what percentage of U.S. college athletes report being 'very lonely' despite being surrounded by teammates?

options.text: Less than 10% — college athletes have strong social support
About 25% — similar to the general population
Over 50% — more than half report being very lonely despite team membership
College athletes don't experience loneliness

questions.explanation: A 2026 Public Health Post review of 194 studies (n=88,516) found that over HALF of U.S. college athletes report being 'very lonely' — despite being surrounded by teammates daily. High-performance sport deepens rather than relieves isolation as competitive intensity increases. This demonstrates that team membership alone doesn't guarantee connection — the QUALITY of the team environment matters, and competitive pressure undermines the social cure mechanisms.

questions.question: Which of the following is a RED FLAG for toxic team culture?

options.text: Coaches use positive, constructive feedback and celebrate improvement
Mistakes are treated as learning opportunities
Fear-based motivation (e.g., 'you'll lose your spot,' 'you're letting us down'), yelling, shaming, or punishment for mistakes
Players feel safe expressing concerns without retaliation

questions.explanation: Fear-based motivation, yelling, shaming, and punishment for mistakes are clear red flags for toxic team culture. Healthy team cultures use positive, constructive feedback, treat mistakes as learning opportunities, and create psychological safety where players can express concerns. Toxic cultures produce performance anxiety, fear of failure, eating disorders, self-harming behaviors, and reduced wellbeing. Recognizing these red flags and leaving unsafe environments is ADAPTIVE, not weak.

questions.question: Under what conditions CAN competitive sport be psychologically healthy?

options.text: Competitive sport is never psychologically healthy

When: participation is genuinely chosen, environment is psychologically safe, performance pressure is balanced with enjoyment, athlete has social connections outside sport, and identity is diversified
Only if the athlete is winning consistently
Competitive sport is always healthy because it builds character

questions.explanation: Competitive sport isn't universally harmful. It CAN be psychologically healthy when: (1) the athlete genuinely CHOOSES participation (not pressured), (2) the environment is psychologically SAFE (supportive coaching, team cohesion, emotional expression allowed), (3) performance pressure is BALANCED with enjoyment and personal development, (4) the athlete has social connections OUTSIDE sport (not isolated), and (5) identity is DIVERSIFIED beyond athletic performance. The risks emerge when these conditions are NOT met — which is unfortunately common in competitive youth and elite sport contexts.

title: The Critical Distinction — Recreational vs. Competitive Sport - Quiz
